texts.full_text: Secondary oxalosis of bone in a dialysis patient. secondary oxalosis of bone is a complication of chronic renal failure. Its frequency and the degree of severity in dialysis patients can vary. The condition is characterized by the accumulation of oxalate crystals in the bone and soft tissues. This accumulation is a result of the body's inability to effectively excrete oxalate due to the reduced kidney function. The reduced kidney function is a hallmark of chronic renal failure and dialysis patients. The condition can lead to a range of symptoms and complications, including bone pain, fractures, and in severe cases, dialysis-related calcification and cardiovascular calcification. The management of secondary oxalosis of bone in dialysis patients is challenging and may involve a combination of dietary oxalate restriction, medications to lower oxalate levels, and in some cases, surgical intervention to remove oxalate crystals. texts.full_text: rom people like Jean Pierre and Beata who survived despite all odds, their resilience was both humbling and inspiring. The experience challenged me as a teacher because history suddenly became more than dates or events; it had faces, voices that echoed through time with raw emotion still intact after two decades. Teaching about genocide before this trip meant lectures filled mostly by facts but lacking emotional depth needed for true understanding and empathy building among students. Coming back home from Rwanda wasn’t easy; I felt changed yet uncertain how best to incorporate these lessons into my classroom practice effectively without overwhelming young minds or causing distress unnecessarily. Over time though, little changes began happening: more emphasis on personal narratives within larger historical contexts during lectures and assignments designed around empathy building exercises such as writing letters from perspectives of different stakeholders involved in conflicts similar to Rwanda's tragedy. These small steps helped bridge gaps between cold hard facts learned through textbooks versus real human experiences captured vividly by survivors’ testimonies at the memorial center. By bringing humanity back into history education, students start seeing past events not just as isolated incidents but rather interconnected threads woven together forming complex tapestries of our collective existence – each thread mattering greatly even if seemingly insignificant alone initially considered under academic scrutiny standards previously adhered strictly before this life-altering journey. texts.full_text: reunion would be like, once the magic is gone. Frankenberry, the Trix Rabbit, Count Chocula, Cap’n Crunch, and Tony The Tiger sit around a dark basement, smoking cigarettes, playing Atari, looking at porn and drinking beer. As oddly amusing as that image is, we prefer to think of these mascots in their cereal peddling heyday. And that leads us to wonder – who is the ‘best’ of these? We've put together a list featuring our personal favorites from this amazing group. 1. Tony The Tiger: This orange and black striped tiger has been representing Kellogg's Frosted Flakes since its debut in 1952, making it one of cereal mascots' most recognizable faces worldwide. 2. Cap'n Crunch (The Sailor): introduced to the breakfast table as Captain Crunch back on October 8th ,1961 by Quaker Oats Company and later changed his name after a legal dispute with Nestlé over their character named "Crunch," this captain of ship has been sailing through our cereals for decades. 3. The Trix Rabbit: A clever rabbit that is the face behind Kellogg's Trix cereal since 1959, he remains one of children’s favorite mascots thanks to his quirky personality and unique appearance. Each character holds a special place in many people hearts due their long history on breakfast tables around America. They have become iconic symbols representing childhood memories th

Post-war societies are marked by the effects of massive, large group traumatization, and if not properly dealt with, long-term rehabilitation and social recovery cannot be expected. Unprocessed trauma that has become deeply embedded in the collective memory of the Balkan peoples over centuries, "chosen trauma", its tranTitle: Trauma Healing Initiative for Post–Balkan War Recovery Introduction/Abstract (150 words): This paper explores a comprehensive approach to addressing deep-rooted traumas within post—war societies using academic tools. The focus is on the Balkan region's unique historical context, characterized by cumulative trauma across generations and its influence of "chosen" or intergenerationally transmitted memories shaping present identities/conflicts.. The model proposed suggests that structured cross_border dialogues between academics can serve as a catalyst for collective healing: - Academic Reconstruction Phase 1 involves collaborative research projects studying war legacies with international scholars. _ Dialogue & Education (Phase II): Cross-cultural educational programs foster understanding, addressing stereotypes via shared historical narratives/perspectives.. Conclusion (~205 words) – 'Bridge Building' through Trauma Awareness...': By implementing these academic interventions in post—conflict Balkan societies we aim at facilitating not only individual but societal transformation regarding traumatic pasts. The system can be described by a set of rate equations for complex electric field amplitude E=E₀exp[iφ] with the phase ϕ, carrier density N , which are coupled to feedback term F(t)=k exp[- iωτ ] . Here k is coupling strength and tau accounts delay. This leads a self-organization phenomenon in laser dynamics: as such short time scale optical perturbations can lead the system towards stable points, where phase φ gets locked to specific values depending on feedback parameters (k , T). Consequently this modifies both amplitude E_0(t) and frequency of emitted light. texts.full_text: [Clinical analysis of 282 patients with non-Hodgkin's lymphoma]. To analyze the classification, stage and prognosis of non-Hodgkin's lymphoma (NHL). Two hundred eighty two patients with NHL were retrospectively reviewed. The complete remission (CR) rate and 5-year survival rate of B-cell NHL were higher than those of T-cell NHL(P < 0.05). There were significant differences in CR rate and 5-year survival rate among the low-grade, the intermediate-grade and the high-grade NHL(P < 0.05). The CR rate and 5-year survival rate of patients whoreceived chemotherapy were higher than those of patients who did not receive chemotherapy (P < 0.05). The results suggest that the classification, stage, and prognosis of NHL are important factors in determining the appropriate treatment and predicting

texts.full_text: s between 402 MHz and 405 MHz, as available for use by medical implants. Although the MICS band represents an attempt at compromise, it is still the case that body tissues significantly attenuate electromagnetic waves propagating at MICS frequencies. As a result, the distance between the base station and the implant must be small. In fact, in many applications, the base station's receiving antenna is placed on or within inches of the skin. The limited range of known medical implant communication systems poses few problems when one wishes to establish communication with an implant infreqThe limited range of known medical implant communication systems poses few problems when one wishes to establish communication with an implant infrequently or when the implant is within the range of the base station. However, the limited range poses significant challenges when the implant is to be monitored or controlled continuously or when the implant is to be used to continuously transmit or receive significant amounts of data. For example, an implant used to continuously monitor the heart or to continuously deliver controlled amounts of medication to the heart or the brain would be of limited use if the range of the implant is limited to the range of the base station, or if the base station is required to be in close proximity to the implant. It is unclear why exactly the peak of the $\chi^2_{\rm{r}}$ distribution is only 0.5, i.e. less than 1. One reason could be that the errors $\sigma_{i}$ of the data are overestimated. The errors returned by the EIS data calibration software are basically Poisson errors, but they also account for other effects such as CCD read-out noise [see @Young+al:2009]. If these errors were overestimated by some 40% (${\approx}1{-}\sqrt{2}$, this would account for the factor of 2 in the $\chi^2_{\rm{r}}$ distribution (cf. Eq. \[E:chi\]). A detailed analysis of the measurement errors of EIS would be needed to fully investigate this, which is beyond the scope of this paper. The assumption of a normal distribution of the errors for the $\chi^2$ test is a potential problem, too. It remains open as to what extent this will influence the distribution of $\chi^2_{\rm{r}}$ and in which direction. Possible problems in the determination of the measurement error and for the underlying assumptions for the $\chi^2$ test show that it is no surprise that the peak of the $\chi^2_{\rm{r}}$ distribution is not at 1. texts.full_text: case of retractile mesenteritis presenting as an abdominal mass with incomplete small-bowel obstruction. Histological features included fat necrosis, fibrosis, elastosis, dystrophic calcification, and chronic inflammation in the perimesenchymal tissues around multiple jejunal segments. Discussion: Retractile (sclerosing) mesenteritis is a rare inflammatory condition characterized by progressive scarring of Meckel's diverticulum with adjacent retroperitoneum, ileocecal region or right lower quadrant. The disease typically presents in children and young adults as an abdominal mass that may cause symptoms such as intermittent pain due to partial bowel obstruction. In this case report, the patient presented with signs consistent with incomplete small-bowel obstruction along with a palpable intra-abdominal lump which initially raised concerns for more sinister pathologies including neoplasms. However, detailed clinical evaluation and subsequent surgical exploration supported by histopathological analysis ultimately led us to diagnose retractile mesenteritis. Histologically, findings of fat necrosis are indicative firstly as partof the inflammatory response mechanism characteristic in many chronic abdominal conditions but especially prominent here given its distribution within perimesenchymal regions around multiple jejunal segments. Moreover fibrotic and elastic changes signifies an advanced stage where reparative healing has been initiated yet without adequate restitution obliterating scars leading to possibly permanent anatomical alterations affecting normal luminal architecture of the bowel wall, facilitating development or exacerbation mechanical obstruction phenomena clinically expressed here through patient's symptoms. Other key histological features like dystrophic calcification further underscores long-standing chronicity often noted in such inflammatory processes while also underlining certain aspects unique according differential diagnosis scenarios involving idiopathic causes against infectious etiologies which typically lack similar ossific deposits unless secondary manifestation of some underlying geno-toxic insult situations not encountered herein. texts.full_text: ia in MRL/lpr Mice]. To study the immune repair effect of umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cells (UC-MSC) on inflammatory disorders and thrombophilia state of MRL/lpr mice by detecting the expression change of peripheral blood CD4(+) CD25(+) T cells and the levels of plasma inflammatory cytokines TNF-α, IL-6 and thrombosis indicators TF, FIB. Twenty five MRL/lpr mice were randomly divided into two groups: control group (n=10), UC-MSC treatment group (UC-MSC) (n=5). The experimental period was set for four weeks with a single dose of intravenous injection in the tail vein. At week one, three and five after transplantation, peripheral blood samples were collected from each mouse to detect CD4(+)CD25(+)T cells by flow cytometry (FCM), plasma inflammatory factors TNF-α, IL-6 levels using ELISA kits. At week four post-transfusion, the mice in UC-MSC group were euthanized to collect blood for TF and FIB level determination by immunohistochemistry (IHC) staining of kidney tissue sections. Results: Compared with control group at baseline: At week one post-transplantation, the percentage of CD4(+)CD25(+)T cells in UC-MSC treatment group increased significantly compared to that before transfusion (P<0.01), while TNF-α and IL-6 levels decreased markedly (both P<0.01). At week three, the expression of CD4(+)CD25(+)T cells continued increasing in UC-MSC group compared to control mice (P<0.05), with further decrease observed for TNF-α and IL-6 levels (both P<0.01). At week five, although there was no significant difference between the two groups regarding CD4(+)CD25(+)T cells percentage anymore due to an increase also seen in control group mice by then; UC-MSC treated animals still maintained lower inflammatory cytokine levels than their counterparts (P<0.01). texts.full_text: lts total or the input doesn’t match any addresses at all, then the system will return just one result with error code: S0021 – Address does not match. Now let’s say you want to get more detailed information about each address returned by using a service like Get Detailed Info for an Address. In this scenario it is best practice to make 3 separate requests of Get Detail Info, one request per matching line from the original lookup. That way your API will stay fast and performant. Now here’s where we can use our service path trick: 1. On each successive request (line) you’ll want to include “?view=results” at the end of that particular URL. 2. Now refresh your page, or better yet hit F5 a few times. You will see the response change on every line as well. What’s happening here is this: With view results appended to each request url we are forcing DOTS to return information about 3 consecutive matching addresses for each line of the original search address. For our purposes that means three responses, with details displayed in a readable fashion. We don’t have to use Get Detailed Info at all! Get detailed info The process above can also be applied to the service GetDetailedInfo (which is how you’re meant to get those kinds of results) by substituting ?view=results for ?&details: Now hit F5 and see what happens. Let’s look closer: First we’re going from Address Verification with ?view=results to a more verbose description in just two clicks. It will probably take 3-4 times as many characters using GetDetailedInfo, but it works much the same way (GetDetailedinfo is a very detailed service). Try another scenario: 1. We start at Address Validation US 2. Then hit F5. 3. Then select “Change key” 4. Finally, add ?view=results to any lookup In order for this method of testing to work you’ll need to be in your DOTS trial account (you won’t have a trial or production key).
